title: Blood on Linen — Cellulose Fiber, Same Rules as Cotton, Different Finishing
stainChemistry: Blood on linen follows the same chemistry as blood on cotton — hemoglobin's protein bonds to cellulose hydroxyl groups, and iron oxidizes from red Fe²⁺ to brown Fe³⁺. Linen (flax) is actually stronger than cotton when wet and tolerates the same pH range (6.0–11.0). The difference is in the finishing: linen wrinkles aggressively, and aggressive wet spotting on linen tablecloths or garments can create permanent press marks if not properly finished while damp. Colored linen can also be less colorfast than cotton due to different dye affinity of flax fiber.
whyThisWorks: Linen is cellulose — it tolerates enzymes, alkaline protein formula, peroxide, and even chlorine bleach on whites. The protocol is essentially identical to blood on white cotton, with one critical finishing difference: linen must be pressed while still slightly damp or it develops permanent creases. Dan Eisen's protein stain sequence (cold flush → lubricant → protein formula → flush → peroxide if needed) applies fully. The enzyme approach is particularly effective because protease only attacks protein (the blood), not cellulose (the linen).

spottingProtocol.technique: Flush from back with water gun
spottingProtocol.temperature: Cold — below 100°F. Same rule as all protein stains.
spottingProtocol.instruction: Place linen face-down on towel over vacuum. Cold water flush from back. Linen is very strong when wet — stronger than cotton — so normal water gun pressure is fine. Watch for red/brown transfer to towel. Linen's more open weave structure means blood may penetrate deeper and faster than cotton, so flush generously.

Apply NSD to remaining blood stain. Linen tolerates brush action — tamp lightly to work lubricant into the weave. Linen's fiber structure is more irregular than cotton, so work the lubricant thoroughly into any slub areas where blood may concentrate.

spottingProtocol.agent: Protein formula (full strength) OR protease enzyme digester
spottingProtocol.temperature: Warm (100–110°F) for enzyme; cool for protein formula
spottingProtocol.dwellTime: 5–15 minutes for enzyme; 3–5 minutes for protein formula
spottingProtocol.instruction: Same approach as cotton. On linen, enzyme digesters work excellently — protease breaks down hemoglobin while leaving cellulose linen fiber untouched. For set/dried blood, enzyme digester with 15-minute dwell is preferred. For fresh blood, chemical protein formula at full strength with 3–5 minute dwell works fast. Tamp periodically.
Flush completely. Inspect: if blood is removed, skip to step 6. If brown shadow persists, continue to step 5.

spottingProtocol.temperature: Warm — steam safe on linen after protein is removed
spottingProtocol.instruction: Apply peroxide with ammonia catalyst to brown iron shadow. Linen tolerates this well — same resilience as cotton. Steam-chase to accelerate the oxidation of Fe³⁺ chromophore. On white linen, you can be aggressive — use 6% professional strength. On colored linen, test first in hidden area.
Neutralize alkaline residue. Critical on linen for the same reason as cotton — residual alkali weakens cellulose chains over time, and linen is expensive to replace.

spottingProtocol.temperature: Cool flush, then hot iron for pressing
spottingProtocol.instruction: LINEN-SPECIFIC: After confirming the stain is gone, linen MUST be pressed while still slightly damp to prevent permanent wrinkle marks from the spotting process. Linen's natural tendency to crease means any wrinkles formed during wet spotting will set permanently if allowed to air-dry first. Press with a hot iron (linen tolerates high heat when protein stain has been fully removed) using steam. Professional: use flatbed press. If stain is fully removed, garment can proceed to normal machine cleaning and finishing.

homeSolutions: FRESH: Run cold water through the back of the linen immediately. Same approach as cotton — cold water, fast action. Follow with enzyme detergent soak (Tide Original in cold water) for 30 minutes. Linen handles this treatment well.

SET/DRIED: Cold enzyme soak for 30+ minutes. Then 3% hydrogen peroxide on the brown shadow — let fizz, blot, repeat. Wash in cold water with enzyme detergent. Iron the treated area while still damp to prevent permanent creasing.

WHITE LINEN TABLECLOTH (the classic scenario): Stretch tablecloth over a bowl, pour cold water through the stain. Then soak entire cloth in cold water + Tide for 1 hour. Hydrogen peroxide on any remaining shadow. For white linen, dilute chlorine bleach (1:10 Clorox) is safe as a last resort — rinse thoroughly.

WHEN TO STOP: If the brown shadow persists after enzyme + peroxide, take it to a professional. Mention that it's linen and needs proper pressing — not all cleaners know that linen requires damp finishing.
materialWarnings: NEVER hot water first — same protein coagulation rule applies. Cold only until blood is fully removed.
NEVER let linen dry wrinkled after spotting — creases set permanently. Press while still damp.
NEVER alcohol on blood — sets protein stains permanently.
NEVER use dryer until stain confirmed gone — heat sets residual iron staining.
NOTE: Colored linen may be less colorfast than cotton — always test any chemistry on a hidden seam before treating the visible stain.

professional.name: Street's Protein Formula
professional.use: Primary protein treatment — full strength safe on linen
professional.note: Linen is cellulose and tolerates full-strength alkaline chemistry. Dwell 3–5 minutes, flush thoroughly, neutralize with acetic acid.

professional.name: Prochem Power Burst
professional.use: Enzyme pre-spray — protease + amylase for set blood
professional.note: Excellent on linen. Protease attacks only the blood protein, not the cellulose fiber. Warm water (100–110°F), dwell 15–20 minutes.

professional.name: Hydrogen Peroxide 6%
professional.use: Iron shadow removal on white linen
professional.note: With ammonia catalyst. Steam-chase safe on linen. White linen tolerates 6% without issue. Test on colored linen first.

consumer.name: Tide Original Liquid
consumer.use: Enzyme detergent soak — cold water, 30+ minutes
consumer.note: The protease enzymes in Tide digest blood protein. Safe on linen (cellulose fiber). Don't use hot water — enzymes denature above 130°F.

consumer.name: Hydrogen Peroxide 3%
consumer.use: Brown iron shadow on white or light linen
consumer.note: Apply directly to shadow, let fizz, blot. Repeat. Safe on white linen. Test colorfastness on colored linen.

consumer.name: OxiClean Versatile Stain Remover
consumer.use: Oxygen bleach soak for linen tablecloths and napkins
consumer.note: Dissolve per label in cold water. Soak linen 1–4 hours. Effective on old blood. Safe on white linen and most colorfast linen. NOT safe on wool or silk.

escalation.when: Blood persists after enzyme treatment + peroxide, blood was heat-set before treatment, stain is on a valuable antique or heirloom linen piece, or you're dealing with a large area (tablecloth soaked through).
escalation.whatToTell: Tell the cleaner: 'This is blood on linen. I treated with cold water and enzyme detergent. A brown shadow remains from the iron in the blood. The fabric was NOT heated. It needs proper damp pressing after treatment — please don't let it dry wrinkled.'
escalation.specialistType: Any competent dry cleaner should handle blood on linen. For heirloom linen or antique tablecloths, seek a cleaner specializing in fine textile care — they'll understand the pressing requirements and handle aged linen (which can be more fragile) with appropriate care.

solventNote: For dry cleaners: Blood on linen is a wet-side pre-spot — cold protein formula, flush, neutralize BEFORE machine cleaning. Linen handles all solvents well (PERC, HC, GreenEarth). The main risk is NOT the solvent — it's pressing. Linen must be finished with steam while still slightly damp. If wrinkle marks form during spotting, they'll be visible after the machine cycle unless properly pressed. Professional tip: on white linen, the combination of enzyme pre-spot + machine clean + post-spot with peroxide produces the best results for old blood.
